method_overview:
Table work is the part of rehearsal where we sit around a table and read the play together before anyone gets up to move. We are not performing yet. We are finding out what the play really is: what happens, who these people are, and what each scene is about.

This is five Thursday evenings in July, working our production of Pirandello's "Six Characters in Search of an Author." We take one act each on the first three nights, then run the whole play, then read it through with cues. The one big note: read to find the play, don't perform it yet. The truthful version comes later, on the floor, in August. Keep it light, keep it moving, and stop talking when talk stops helping.

room_norms: We read to discover, not to correct. Nobody locks in a fixed "take" on a character or a line this early.
We stay seated all five evenings. No moving, no blocking, no physical or intimacy work at the table. The big "over-the-top" read pushes the voice only, never touch.
The charged moments (the veil, the shop scene, the deaths) are talked about in words only. We set them as safe, agreed choreography in August, never improvised.
Read first, talk second. The director asks what you found before offering an interpretation.
Anyone can say "hold" at any moment, for any reason, no explanation needed. We agree this word on night one.
Don't over-talk it. Every extra minute of table chat is a staging minute lost. Share the facts and the questions, then we get up in August.
roles: Director (Kiarash Jamshidi) — leads each evening, runs the two readings and the chats after, and keeps the tone honest: funny and painful at once.
Assistant Director — takes notes, tracks the decisions we make, flags when the tone drifts or energy drops, and fills in anyone who missed a night.
Stage Manager — booked the room, handed out scripts, keeps attendance and the prompt book, reads stage directions aloud, watches the clock, and calls cues on night five.
Everyone — read aloud, join both readings, fill in your homework sheet, and speak up. Quiet roles get asked the same questions as the leads.
materials: The rehearsal script, handed out by the Stage Manager before night one.
A pencil and a simple two-column sheet: Facts on one side, Questions on the other.
A one-page character sheet: Who am I? What just happened? My relationships? What do I want? What's in my way? What do I do about it?
A short list of strong verbs for actioning (one verb per line: what am I doing to the other person? — e.g. shock, reassure, tease, threaten).
The two stage objects: the Child-bundle (a white wrapped cloth) and the Boy-chair (a chair, coat, cap, satchel). They sit with the Mother and Step-Daughter from night one.
Water, tea, and a snack for the breaks. Name tags for anyone new on night one.
how_to_run.cadence: Each evening runs 18:00 to 21:00 at SSA Lausanne, everyone seated, with a real break in the middle and a short second break later. We start with a quick check-in and a gentle vocal warm-up, do the main reading and work, then a fast go-round so everyone speaks, set homework, and finish with a short check-out.

principles.name: The two-pass read: flat first, over-the-top second
principles.what: We open each act with two opposite reads of its key scenes: once completely flat (just the words, no emotion), then once way too big (push everything to the extreme).
principles.why: The flat read lets us hear what's actually there and stops anyone fixing a line too soon; the big read finds the ceiling, so the honest version is easy to find later.

principles.name: Read to discover, then get up
principles.what: Read the scene through before we discuss it, and do only as much table work as we need to agree the facts and the questions.
principles.why: Too much sitting and analysing traps actors in their heads; the real discoveries happen on your feet, so we save them for August.

principles.name: Funny and painful in the same breath
principles.what: Don't play it as a farce (the wounds become jokes) or as cold philosophy (the feeling goes dead). Earn the laugh, then let it turn to a lump in the throat.
principles.why: This play only works when the audience laughs and then wishes they hadn't. Get the tone wrong and the whole thing falls apart.

principles.name: Know which layer of the play you're in
principles.what: This is a play within a play. Name out loud who you are: an actor playing an actor, the tired stage Manager, one of the six trapped Characters, or Madame Pace.
principles.why: The play runs on the clash between these layers — who is more real — so you have to know which one you're standing in at any moment.

principles.name: Aim outward, not inward
principles.what: For each line, name what you want from the other person and pick one strong verb for it (actioning: what am I doing to them?). Turn any "I feel..." into "what am I doing, and to whom?"
principles.why: Feeling comes out of doing. Aiming at the other person keeps the Father from lecturing and the Step-Daughter sharp instead of just angry.

sessions.title: Act One at the table
sessions.focus: Settle in calmly: agree the ground rules, read Act One flat and then over-the-top, talk about what jumped out, and take a short plain look at the world of the play.
goals: Agree the ground rules and the time-out word, quickly and lightly.
Hear Act One two ways (flat, then over-the-top) and test whether "Valentine" lands as a wound or just a joke.
Get a clear, plain picture of who these people are and how the play-within-a-play works.

agenda.detail: One sentence each: what you need to take part fully tonight.
A gentle seated voice and breath warm-up.
Agree the time-out word, the check-in and the de-role check-out. Quick and light.
Short, plain talk: who the family and the company are, and the play-within-a-play.
Read it flat, then put the Part I questions below. (See “Part by part”.)
Read it flat, then the Part II questions. (The six walk in.)
Read it flat, then the Part III questions.
Water, tea, food. Rest your voice.
Read the whole act way too big — full melodrama, no holding back.
Where it got funny, dangerous or raw — and where “Valentine” stops being just a song.
Set homework, do the de-role check-out, confirm 9 July is Act Two.

exercises.name: The flat read
exercises.how: Read the key scenes with zero emotion, just words and punctuation. If anyone starts colouring it, speed up to a fast, throwaway pace. Go part by part; after each part’s flat read, put the part’s questions to the characters in it — one sentence each, in role.
exercises.purpose: Lets the room hear what's really in Act One and stops anyone locking a line too early.

exercises.name: The over-the-top read
exercises.how: Read the same scenes far too big, full permission, oversized voice. Push "Valentine" hard. Seated, no touch.
exercises.purpose: Finds the ceiling and shows where the comedy and the wounds actually live, so the honest version is findable later.

discussion_prompts: After the flat read: what is actually happening in Act One, and what did you assume that wasn't there?
After the big read: where did it get funny, dangerous or raw, and did "Valentine" feel like a wound?
Who are these people to each other, and which laughs should cost us something?
sessions.homework: Fill in your character sheet for your Act One scene and start learning your Act One lines.
sessions.outcome: We've agreed the ground rules, shared a clear picture of the play, and heard Act One both ways with "Valentine" tested as a wound.

questions.ask: What kind of day are you having before they walk in?
What do you think you’re owed in this company?
What keeps you patient with all of them?
What are you still excited about?

parts.name: Part II — The Interruption
parts.present: The Father, The Step-Daughter, The Mother, The Son, The Manager

questions.ask: You’ve walked into a stranger’s theatre — what do you need them to believe?
What do you want this room to see that the Father won’t say?
What are you trying to stop from happening?
Why don’t you want to be here?
When do you stop wanting to throw them out?

parts.name: Part III — The Bargain
parts.present: The Father, The Manager, The Step-Daughter

questions.ask: What are you offering him — and what’s the catch you’re hiding?
What finally makes you say yes?
What are you afraid they’ll make pretty?

sessions.title: Act Two at the table
sessions.focus: Read Act Two flat and over-the-top, find why the comedy after Madame Pace has to be nervous, and place the Mother's quiet keystone line.
goals: Hear Act Two two ways and feel why the comedy must stay nervous, not a funny show.
Build Madame Pace so the laugh turns sour by her last line, and hear the music.
Place and protect the Mother's quiet line "It's taking place now" so it isn't drowned out.

agenda.detail: One sentence each, then a seated voice warm-up. The pianist is here tonight.
Read it flat, then the Part I questions. Includes the Step-Daughter’s shower speech.
Read it flat, then the Part II questions. Madame Pace arrives.
Read it flat, then the Part III questions. The actors replay the scene.
Rest your voice. Water and food.

agenda.activity: Act Two, over-the-top — the comedy test
agenda.detail: Read it too big and overplay the comedy after Pace — feel exactly where the laugh has to curdle.

agenda.activity: Madame Pace and the curdle point
agenda.detail: Funny accent on arrival, full menace by the end. Mark where the room must stop laughing.

agenda.activity: The music and the Mother’s keystone
agenda.detail: Pianist marks the Satie and the cabaret vamp; place “It’s taking place now.”
One thing each; homework; de-role check-out.

exercises.name: The two reads of Act Two
exercises.how: Read the key scenes flat first, then way too big with the comedy pushed to the limit. Seated, no touch. Go part by part; after each part’s flat read, put the part’s questions to the characters in it — one sentence each, in role.
exercises.purpose: The flat read shows the act's real shape; the big read shows exactly why the comedy can't stay a funny show.

exercises.name: Marking the curdle point
exercises.how: Read Madame Pace's scene and, as a room, pencil the exact word where the laugh turns to disgust, and where the music dies mid-bar.
exercises.purpose: Locks the funny-then-painful tone of the most delicate scene before we ever stage it.

discussion_prompts: When you pushed the comedy too far, where did it stop being funny and start being frightening?
Where is Madame Pace funny, and where do we first wish we hadn't laughed?
What keeps the Mother's quiet line from being drowned out by everything around it?
sessions.homework: Bring a character sheet for your Act Two scene; Madame Pace, know the lines cold; Mother, rehearse "It's taking place now" aloud.
sessions.outcome: We've heard Act Two both ways, agreed the comedy must be nervous, built Madame Pace, heard the music, and protected the Mother's quiet line.

parts.name: Part I — The Setup
parts.present: The Step-Daughter, The Manager, The Mother, The Son

questions.ask: In the shower speech, who are you really talking to?
What are you actually building out of these props?
What do you dread is coming?
What are you watching from the edge?

parts.name: Part II — The Apparition
parts.present: Madame Pace, The Step-Daughter, The Father, The Mother

questions.ask: What are you really selling?
What is it like to watch this happen again?
What do you tell yourself so you can walk into that shop?
What finally breaks your silence?

parts.name: Part III — The Substitution
parts.present: Player 1, Player 2, The Step-Daughter, The Mother

questions.ask: How do you think the scene is going?
What do you get wrong without knowing it?
What’s unbearable about watching them play you?
What does “It’s taking place now” mean to you?

sessions.title: Act Three at the table
sessions.focus: Read Act Three flat and over-the-top, find the line between real feeling and cold argument, and protect the quiet around the two deaths.
goals: Hear Act Three two ways and find where the Father is panicking, not lecturing.
Work the Son's refusal and his cry on the word "horrible," and the fountain story.
Protect the quiet, especially the silence after the gunshot, and talk through the staged moments in words only.

agenda.detail: One sentence each, seated warm-up. The Mother keeps the Child-bundle and Boy-chair by her.
Read it flat, then the Part I questions. The reality/illusion argument.
Read it flat, then the Part II questions. The Son will not act.
Read it flat, then the Part III questions. The two deaths.
Rest your voice — this is the heaviest act.

agenda.activity: Act Three, over-the-top — feeling vs argument
agenda.detail: Read it too big, overshoot both raw feeling and cold philosophy, then find the line between them.

agenda.activity: The Father’s argument and the Son’s cry
agenda.detail: Score the Father control → horror as panic, not lecture; let the Son’s cry land on “horrible.”
Read the ending for stillness, not volume; talk the staged moments through — no contact at the table.
One-line Act Three aim each; homework; de-role check-out.

exercises.name: The two reads of Act Three
exercises.how: Read the key scenes flat, giving the quiet its air, then too big, overshooting both feeling and cold argument. Go part by part; after each part’s flat read, put the part’s questions to the characters in it — one sentence each, in role.
exercises.purpose: Finds the live line the act runs on and protects the quiet moments around the deaths.

exercises.name: The mirror-speech surprise read
exercises.how: The Son reads the speech twice: once trying to reach the feeling early, then finding the cry only on "horrible."
exercises.purpose: Shows why the cry has to surprise him mid-thought, not be loaded up in advance.

discussion_prompts: When you overshot both feeling and philosophy, where did the true line turn out to be?
Did the quiet survive even the big read: the cry on "horrible," the silence after the gunshot, the fountain?
For the staged moments, what carries the weight so the bodies don't have to, and what do we need to agree now?
sessions.homework: Father, bring your arc with a strong verb per section; Son, learn the fountain story as one breath; Mother, decide the shape of your discovery with the veil never lifting.
sessions.outcome: We've heard Act Three both ways, found the line between feeling and argument, protected the quiet, and agreed how the staged moments are handled.

parts.name: Part I — The Trap
parts.present: The Father, The Step-Daughter, The Manager, The Mother

questions.ask: What are you really arguing for when you talk about what’s real?
When do you cut him off — and why then?
When does this stop being a game to you?
What are you holding onto?

parts.name: Part II — The Refusal
parts.present: The Son, The Father, The Manager, The Step-Daughter

questions.ask: What will you not do, and why?
Why do you need him to play it?
What do you do when an actor refuses?
Why are you sure he can’t leave?

parts.name: Part III — The Fountain
parts.present: The Son, The Mother, The Step-Daughter, The Manager

questions.ask: What did you see at the fountain?
What do your empty arms know before anyone says it?
Why do you run?
Pretence or reality — what do you walk out believing?

sessions.title: The whole play, joined up
sessions.focus: Read the play as one connected thing, in words only: relationships across the arc, the layers, the tempo, the nervous comedy, and the safety agreement confirmed.
goals: Follow each relationship right across the play instead of act by act.
Place every performer in their layer and track where "who is more real" flips.
Confirm the tempo, the nervous comedy, and every safety agreement, all in words.

agenda.detail: One sentence each, then a seated warm-up. The objects in place. The director frames tonight: the whole play in one room, in words, nothing staged.
Trace each thread across all three acts: the Father explains, the Step-Daughter exposes, the Son refuses, the Mother carries. Where does each one turn?
Place everyone in their layer again and track the moments where who-is-more-real flips: the casting scene, Madame Pace, the keystone line, the deaths.
Rest your voice. Water and food.
Walk the changes of pace in order: the company's looseness against the family's drive, the brisk casting scene, the ruptures around the two deaths.
Walk the funny beats across the play and agree where each laugh has to turn. "Valentine" stays a wound, not a number.
Short breather. Rest your voice.
Walk the full list of contact moments. Each is set safely in August with someone else present. The deaths stay object work; consent can always be withdrawn.
Everyone names one thing about their whole arc and one thing they need to do it safely. Set homework and check out.

exercises.name: Follow one thread
exercises.how: Take each main relationship and read or summarise its key beats across all three acts in one go, marking where it turns and where it risks getting lost.
exercises.purpose: Lets you feel each relationship as one continuous line, not three separate readings.

exercises.name: Name your layer
exercises.how: Go round the table at each big clash and have each performer say which layer they're in and what's pressing on it right then.
exercises.purpose: Keeps the play-within-a-play alive all the way through, not just named on night one.

discussion_prompts: Read whole, did each thread stay alive, or did one get rolled over when the play ran end to end?
Where does "who is more real" flip, and can you name your layer at that exact moment?
Is our safety agreement strong enough to carry every contact moment into August?
sessions.homework: Re-read your whole part against tonight's notes, holding your thread as one line, and keep learning Act One toward off-book on 20 August.
sessions.outcome: We've held the whole play in one room in words: threads traced, layers placed, tempo and comedy confirmed, and every safety agreement reconfirmed.

sessions.title: The full cue-called read, and hand-off to August
sessions.focus: Read the whole play through with the Stage Manager calling cues and the pianist marking tempo, then hand off cleanly to the August staging block.
goals: Run the whole play as one cued read, giving Act Two its full time.
Feel the tempo, the relationships, and the cues land end to end.
Confirm the safety agreement and hand off to August with the calendar set.

agenda.detail: Check-in and warm-up. Objects in place, pianist ready. Get logistics out of the way now: a quick safety recap and the August calendar, so the read runs unbroken.
Read Act One straight through, seated, with the Stage Manager calling lights and sound and "Valentine" sung or marked. Earn the laugh, then let it curdle.
A short rest after the unbroken Act One read, before Act Two, so no stretch of reading runs too long. Water, quiet, no talking needed.
Read Act Two straight through with full time, the SM calling cues and the pianist marking the Satie and the vamp. Keep the casting scene brisk.
Rest your voice. Water and food.
Read Act Three straight through, the SM calling the cello, the gunshot in real silence, and the Pärt at the end. Leave the silence after the shot alone.
The director gives a few quick, practical notes: where the pace flattened, where a clash needs sharpening, where a thread read clearly.
Final check-out for the table-work block. The SM confirms the August calendar and the Act One off-book date, and the director thanks everyone.

exercises.name: The cued read
exercises.how: Read the whole play in one sitting (with the break and the voice rest) while the SM calls every cue and the pianist marks tempo. Give Act Two its full time. No stopping for fixes; save notes for the end.
exercises.purpose: Lets the company feel the shape of the show end to end, with the cues in place, before we get on our feet.

exercises.name: "Valentine" in context
exercises.how: Run the "Valentine" moment inside the full Act One read, right after we've heard she's orphaned and for sale, and note exactly where the laugh should curdle.
exercises.purpose: Confirms "Valentine" as a wound, not a comic number, under a full cued run.

discussion_prompts: Across the whole run, where did the tempo flatten, and where did the clashes land as real ruptures?
Did the comedy stay nervous all the way through, or did it tip into farce anywhere?
With the cues in place, did the Mother's quiet lines survive when we ran it whole?
sessions.homework: Have Act One off-book by 20 August and learn the rest across the summer; arrive ready to be on your feet.
sessions.outcome: We've read the whole play through with cues, felt its shape end to end, confirmed the comedy and the safety agreement, and handed off to August.

pitfalls: Skipping the flat read because you want to "get on with it." The flat read is the important one; protect it.
Treating the over-the-top read as the goal. It's a tool to find the ceiling, not the performance. Run it once, talk it over, then let the size go.
Directing from page one. Read first, talk second. Ask the room what they found before you offer a reading.
Letting the comedy win. If the laugh never turns sour, the wounds become jokes. Keep it nervous, never a punchline.
Over-talking it. Five evenings is tight. When talk stops helping, stop talking and save it for the floor.
Letting the quiet roles go silent. Ask the Mother, the Son and the Players the same questions as the leads, every night.

sources.title: Our production's director's edition of "Six Characters in Search of an Author"
sources.author: Luigi Pirandello, adapted and directed by Kiarash Jamshidi
sources.note: For all our specific choices: the characters, the music, the objects, the layers, and the schedule.

sources.title: Our Intimacy and Consent agreement
sources.author: Kiarash Jamshidi / Village Players
sources.note: For how we keep the charged scenes safe and consented.

sources.title: The Director's Craft
sources.author: Katie Mitchell
sources.note: For building the facts, questions and world of the play before we interpret it.

sources.title: Stanislavski's system, with units, objectives and through-line
sources.author: Konstantin Stanislavski (via Maria Knebel)
sources.note: For the units and objectives, and for the rule that we get up and act rather than over-analyse.

sources.title: Actions: The Actors' Thesaurus
sources.author: Marina Caldarone & Maggie Lloyd-Williams
sources.note: For actioning: one strong verb per line, what am I doing to the other person.

sources.title: On Humor (L'Umorismo)
sources.author: Luigi Pirandello
sources.note: For the tone we live on: earn the laugh, then let it curdle.
